events.text: Patient is a 70-year-old White male, height 5'10", weight 192 lbs, BMI 27.6, former smoker with 15 pack-year history, history of hyperlipidemia, no family history of prostate cancer, retired police officer.
At age 60, patient was diagnosed with prostate adenocarcinoma (ICD-O site C61.9, histology 8140/3) after an elevated PSA of 8.4 ng/mL on routine screening; digital rectal exam was normal.

At age 60, transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate biopsy showed Gleason 4+3=7 (Grade Group 3) adenocarcinoma in 5 of 12 cores with perineural invasion; predominantly from the right apex and mid-gland.
At age 60, staging CT and bone scan were negative for metastatic disease; MRI showed no extraprostatic extension; staged as cT2bN0M0.
At age 60, patient underwent robot-assisted radical prostatectomy with bilateral pelvic lymph node dissection; pathologic stage pT3aN0M0 with focal positive margin at the right apex.

At age 60, prostatectomy specimen showed Gleason 4+3=7 adenocarcinoma with focal extraprostatic extension at the right apex, positive apical margin over 3 mm, negative margins elsewhere, and 0 of 16 lymph nodes positive.
At age 60, postoperative PSA at 6 weeks was 0.04 ng/mL; given the positive margin, close surveillance with PSA every 3 months was planned.
At age 62, PSA rose to 0.42 ng/mL confirming biochemical recurrence; considered for salvage therapy.
At age 62, salvage radiation therapy was delivered to the prostate bed, total dose 68 Gy in 34 fractions, with concurrent ADT.
At age 62, leuprolide 22.5 mg intramuscular every 3 months was initiated with salvage radiation, planned for 24 months given high-risk recurrence features.
At age 63, PSA nadir on ADT was less than 0.03 ng/mL; testosterone suppressed to 12 ng/dL.
At age 64, leuprolide was discontinued after completing 24 months; PSA remained undetectable.
At age 66, PSA rose to 1.8 ng/mL with testosterone recovered to 260 ng/dL; imaging was ordered.
At age 66, PSMA PET/CT showed a single PSMA-avid lesion in the right iliac bone (SUVmax 9.2); no other sites of disease.
At age 66, leuprolide 45 mg intramuscular every 6 months was restarted for oligometastatic recurrence.
At age 66, stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) 35 Gy in 5 fractions was delivered to the right iliac bone metastasis for metastasis-directed therapy.
At age 67, PSA declined to 0.3 ng/mL after ADT and SBRT to oligometastatic lesion; testosterone was 18 ng/dL.

At age 68, PSMA PET/CT showed treated right iliac lesion with decreased uptake but new PSMA-avid lesions in L3 vertebral body, left sacrum, and bilateral proximal femora; progressive metastatic disease.
At age 68, PSA was 7.4 ng/mL with testosterone of 20 ng/dL on continuous leuprolide; metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer confirmed.
At age 68, disease reclassified as metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer with progressive polymetastatic bone disease while on continuous ADT.
At age 68, enzalutamide 160 mg orally daily was added to continued leuprolide for mCRPC.

At age 68, next-generation sequencing (FoundationOne CDx) on archival prostatectomy tissue revealed ATM homozygous deletion, TMPRSS2-ERG fusion, TP53 C176Y mutation, and PTEN heterozygous loss; BRCA1/2 wild type; tumor mutational burden 4 mutations/Mb; microsatellite stable.
At age 69, PSA declined to 2.1 ng/mL on enzalutamide; bone pain was controlled with naproxen.
At age 69, patient developed grade 2 hypertension (BP 156/94 mmHg) on enzalutamide requiring addition of hydrochlorothiazide to his regimen.
At age 70, restaging bone scan showed progressive disease with new lesions in bilateral ribs and left proximal humerus; PSA was 9.8 ng/mL.
At age 70, enzalutamide was discontinued and docetaxel 75 mg/m2 intravenously every 3 weeks with prednisone 5 mg twice daily was started.
At age 70, after cycle 4 of docetaxel, patient developed grade 2 peripheral neuropathy with numbness in hands and feet, grade 2 fatigue, and grade 1 nail changes; dose reduced to 60 mg/m2.

scenario_blurb: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). Rising PSA on ADT. Treated with enzalutamide, abiraterone, docetaxel, or cabazitaxel. Include BRCA1/2 and homologous recombination deficiency testing. Some with bone-predominant and some with visceral metastases.
